title: Κάλαμος καὶ ἐλαία
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 143 (= Perry 70). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Διὰ καρτερίαν καὶ ἰσχὺν καὶ ἡσυχίαν κάλαμος καὶ ἐλαία ἤριζον.
sentences.tr: Dia karterian kai ischyn kai hēsychian kalamos kai elaia ērizon.
sentences.l: Over endurance and strength and steadiness a reed and an olive-tree were-quarrelling.
sentences.i: A reed and an olive tree were disputing over endurance, strength, and steadiness.
sentences.n: The familiar quarrel-opening (ἤριζον) — here with διά + accusative naming the stakes instead of περί + genitive. In the English tradition this fable belongs to the oak; Chambry's Greek pits reed against olive.

sentences.t: Τοῦ δὲ καλάμου ὀνειδιζομένου ὑπὸ τῆς ἐλαίας ὡς ἀδυνάτου καὶ ῥᾳδίως ὑποκλινομένου πᾶσι τοῖς ἀνέμοις, ὁ κάλαμος σιωπῶν οὐκ ἐφθέγξατο.
sentences.tr: Tou de kalamou oneidizomenou hypo tēs elaias hōs adynatou kai rhāidiōs hypoklinomenou pasi tois anemois, ho kalamos siōpōn ouk ephthenxato.
sentences.l: And the reed being-taunted by the olive as powerless and easily bending-under to-all the winds, the reed, keeping-silent, uttered nothing.
sentences.i: The olive taunted the reed as a weakling that bowed to every wind; the reed kept silent and said not a word.
sentences.n: ὡς ἀδυνάτου — the accusation-ὡς again ('on the ground that it was…'), agreeing with the reed inside the genitive absolute. The reed's silence is the fable's hinge: it will answer with physics, not words.

sentences.t: Καὶ μικρὸν ὑπομείνας, ἐπειδὴ ἄνεμος ἔπνευσεν ἰσχυρός, ὁ μὲν κάλαμος ὑποσεισθεὶς καὶ ὑποκλινθεὶς τοῖς ἀνέμοις ῥᾳδίως διεσώθη· ἡ δ' ἐλαία, ἐπειδὴ ἀντέτεινε τοῖς ἀνέμοις, κατεκλάσθη τῇ βίᾳ.
sentences.tr: Kai mikron hypomeinas, epeidē anemos epneusen ischyros, ho men kalamos hyposeistheis kai hypoklintheis tois anemois rhāidiōs diesōthē: hē d' elaia, epeidē anteteine tois anemois, kateklasthē tēi biāi.
sentences.l: And a little having-waited, when a wind blew strong, the reed, shaken-under and bent-under to-the winds, easily came-through-safe: but the olive, since she-strained-against the winds, was-snapped by-the force.
sentences.i: A little while later a strong wind blew up; the reed, swaying and bending before it, came through easily — but the olive, because it strained against the winds, was snapped by their force.
sentences.n: The μέν/δέ pair delivers the verdict: ὑποκλινθείς … διεσώθη against ἀντέτεινε … κατεκλάσθη — bend and survive, resist and break. The two ὑπο- compounds (shaken-under, bent-under) are the reed's whole strategy in prefix form.

sentences.t: Ὁ μῦθος δηλοῖ ὅτι οἱ τῷ καιρῷ καὶ τοῖς κρείττοσιν αὐτῶν μὴ ἀνθιστάμενοι κρείττους εἰσὶ τῶν πρὸς μείζονας φιλονεικούντων.
sentences.tr: Ho mythos dēloi hoti hoi tōi kairōi kai tois kreittosin autōn mē anthistamenoi kreittous eisi tōn pros meizonas philoneikountōn.
sentences.l: The fable shows that those to-the occasion and to-the stronger than-them not standing-against are stronger than-those against greater-ones contending.
sentences.i: The fable shows that those who do not fight the moment and their superiors fare better than those who contend with the stronger.
sentences.n: A play on κρείττων: those who yield τοῖς κρείττοσιν, 'to the stronger,' end up κρείττους, 'stronger,' than the strugglers. The moral's paradox is built from one comparative used twice.
